evidence.supports_claim: The compromised priv_lvl_o flows directly out of the CSR regfile and is exposed at the ariane top-level boundary.

finding.reasoning_summary: The CSR regfile computes `priv_lvl_o` as `(debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q`. The `umode_i` input is an unguarded external port with no authentication, no lock register, and no lifecycle qualification. Any agent capable of asserting `umode_i` can force the CPU's reported privilege level to M-mode, which: (1) suppresses virtual address translation (`en_translation_o` checks `priv_lvl_o != PRIV_LVL_M`), (2) affects trap delegation and CSR accessibility, and (3) is externally visible via `priv_lvl_o`. This is a missing privilege guard vulnerability.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted hardware module that controls the `umode_i` input can silently escalate the CPU's effective privilege level to machine mode, disabling MMU-based memory isolation for the entire core. This breaks the U/S/M privilege separation fundamental to RISC-V security.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of `umode_i` (what drives it at the SoC level) is not present in the provided snippets. If `umode_i` is only asserted by a trusted, authenticated hardware root-of-trust with appropriate lifecycle controls, the severity would be reduced. However, within the RTL boundary shown, there is no such guard visible.

finding.summary: The SATP CSR (satp_ppn_o / satp_ppn_csr_ex) can be read back by Supervisor-mode software without any privilege check on the read path, and the write path for CSR_SATP lacks a TVM (Trap Virtual Memory) guard, allowing S-mode to update the page-table root without trapping when mstatus.TVM=1.

evidence.description: CSR_SATP read path returns satp_q directly (line 209) with no privilege level check and no check of mstatus.TVM, exposing the physical page-number and ASID to any privilege level that can issue a CSR read.
evidence.supports_claim: The SATP register is readable without a privilege or TVM guard in the read path.

evidence.description: The CSR_SATP write handler (lines 429-435) updates satp_d after sanitising the ASID and mode fields, but never checks mstatus.TVM. RISC-V privileged spec §3.1.6.3 requires that when TVM=1, S-mode attempts to read or write satp raise an illegal-instruction exception; neither the read (SNIP-0003) nor the write (SNIP-0008) path enforces this.
evidence.supports_claim: S-mode software can write SATP (changing the page-table root) even when mstatus.TVM=1, bypassing the hypervisor/M-mode isolation control.

evidence.description: The en_translation_o signal correctly gates on priv_lvl_o != PRIV_LVL_M, and satp_ppn_o is assigned directly from satp_q.ppn (line 944) with no further gating. The absence of a TVM-based exception on the write path means the PPN value fed to the MMU can be attacker-controlled from S-mode.
evidence.supports_claim: The physical page-number reaching the MMU (and forwarded as satp_ppn_csr_ex) is not protected against S-mode manipulation when TVM=1.

evidence.description: satp_ppn_csr_ex (44-bit PPN) is forwarded from the CSR file to the execute stage and ultimately controls virtual-to-physical address translation. An S-mode attacker that can write SATP despite TVM=1 can redirect all page-table walks to attacker-controlled memory.
evidence.supports_claim: Exploiting the missing TVM guard lets an S-mode guest remap its own address space, potentially escaping a hypervisor sandbox.

finding.reasoning_summary: RISC-V spec requires that when mstatus.TVM=1, S-mode accesses to CSR_SATP raise an illegal-instruction exception, giving M-mode / hypervisors control over when S-mode may change its address-translation root. The csr_regfile write case for CSR_SATP (SNIP-0008, lines 429-435) performs ASID masking and mode validation but contains no check of the current privilege level or mstatus.TVM. Similarly, the read case (SNIP-0003, line 209) returns satp_q unconditionally. The tvm_o output (SNIP-0010, line 952) is produced for the decode/ID stage to gate SFENCE.VMA, but the CSR read/write permission logic inside csr_regfile itself does not consult it for SATP accesses. As a result, S-mode software can freely read and write SATP regardless of the TVM bit, and the 44-bit PPN propagates to satp_ppn_csr_ex and into the MMU.
finding.security_impact: An S-mode attacker (e.g., a malicious OS running under a hypervisor) can repoint the page-table root to arbitrary physical memory by writing SATP even when the hypervisor has set TVM=1 to prevent this. This breaks hypervisor-enforced memory isolation and can lead to arbitrary physical-memory access or privilege escalation.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet set does not include the privilege-level access-check logic (the section that raises illegal-instruction on insufficient privilege), so it is possible that a separate gate elsewhere in csr_update checks privilege and TVM before dispatching to the SATP case. However, the visible write handler (SNIP-0008) and read handler (SNIP-0003) show no such check, and tvm_o is only routed externally rather than used inside the CSR file itself.

finding.summary: The `set_debug_pc_o` signal in csr_regfile is driven without any privilege-level check, and the `debug_req` signal passed into csr_regfile is only gated by `~amo_valid_commit`, with no authentication or privilege verification before entering debug mode or setting the debug PC.

evidence.description: In ariane.sv, `debug_req` is computed as `debug_req_i & ~amo_valid_commit`. The only gate on the external debug request is the AMO-in-progress flag. No privilege level, authentication token, or lock register is checked before the debug request is forwarded to the CSR file.
evidence.supports_claim: The debug request path has a single, easily-satisfied gate (no active AMO) and no privilege or authentication guard.

evidence.description: In csr_regfile.sv at line 284, `set_debug_pc_o` is unconditionally defaulted to 0 and then asserted elsewhere in the combinational block driven by `debug_req_i` without any check of the current privilege level or a secure enable bit. The debug PC redirect can be triggered purely by asserting `debug_req_i`.
evidence.supports_claim: set_debug_pc_o can be asserted by any agent capable of asserting debug_req_i without privilege enforcement.

evidence.description: At line 938, `priv_lvl_o` is overridden to PRIV_LVL_M whenever `debug_mode_q` is set or `umode_i` is asserted. Once debug mode is entered (triggered by the ungated debug_req), the processor executes at machine privilege level, giving full access to all CSRs and memory.
evidence.supports_claim: Entering debug mode via the weakly-guarded debug_req path immediately escalates execution privilege to M-mode.

evidence.description: At line 545, exceptions in debug mode do not update CSR fields (`if (!debug_mode_q && ex_i.valid)`). This means that once debug mode is entered, normal exception-based privilege enforcement is bypassed.
evidence.supports_claim: Debug mode suppresses normal exception/privilege update mechanisms, compounding the impact of unauthorized debug entry.

evidence.description: At line 269, counters and state updates are suppressed during debug mode (`if (!debug_mode_q)`), showing that debug_mode_q is a broad control signal affecting system state, entered through the weakly-guarded debug request path.
evidence.supports_claim: debug_mode_q affects broad system behavior and is reachable via a path with insufficient access control.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `debug_req_i` input is forwarded to the CSR register file after only being masked by `~amo_valid_commit` in ariane.sv. There is no privilege-level check, no authentication mechanism, and no lock register governing whether debug mode entry is permitted. Upon receiving a debug request, the CSR file sets `set_debug_pc_o` and transitions `debug_mode_q` high, which then forces `priv_lvl_o` to PRIV_LVL_M (machine mode) regardless of the actual current privilege level. This allows any agent with access to the `debug_req_i` pin to escalate the processor to machine privilege and redirect the program counter to the debug ROM without any authorization check.
finding.security_impact: An untrusted or compromised agent capable of asserting `debug_req_i` can force the processor into debug mode, immediately gaining machine-level privilege, suppressing normal exception handling, and redirecting execution to the debug ROM. This constitutes a complete privilege escalation and arbitrary code execution path under machine privilege.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The snippet showing the exact combinational logic that asserts `set_debug_pc_o` in response to `debug_req_i` is not present in the provided snippets (the relevant always_comb block body is only partially shown). The analysis relies on the default assignment at line 284 and the known RISC-V debug spec behavior. If there is an additional privilege guard in the unshown portion of the csr_update block, the severity could be lower.

finding.summary: The CSR_DCSR register can be written without verifying that the CPU is in debug mode, allowing unprivileged or non-debug software to modify the dcsr.step (single-step) bit and other sensitive debug control fields.

evidence.description: At line 377-381, the CSR_DCSR write handler unconditionally accepts csr_wdata[31:0] into dcsr_d without checking debug_mode_q. Any instruction that passes the privilege/write-enable check (csr_we) can overwrite the entire DCSR register, including the step bit and prv field.
evidence.supports_claim: Missing debug_mode_q guard on CSR_DCSR write path allows non-debug-mode software to modify debug control state.

evidence.description: At line 961, single_step_o is directly derived from dcsr_q.step. If dcsr_q is modified outside debug mode, single-step behavior is corrupted or can be enabled by an attacker.
evidence.supports_claim: dcsr_q.step controls the single_step_o output, making DCSR a security-sensitive register that requires debug-mode-only write access.

evidence.description: single_step_csr_commit is declared in ariane.sv and connected to the single_step_o output of csr_regfile. This signal controls commit-stage single-stepping behavior, making it a high-value target.
evidence.supports_claim: The single_step_csr_commit signal is fed directly from dcsr_q.step via single_step_o, confirming the impact path of an unauthorized DCSR write.

evidence.description: At line 938, priv_lvl_o is forced to PRIV_LVL_M in debug mode, but the write to CSR_DCSR at line 377 is gated only by csr_we (the general write-enable), not by debug_mode_q. The privilege elevation in debug mode does not compensate for missing debug-mode enforcement on the write side.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege level output correctly reflects debug mode, but the write guard for DCSR does not enforce debug_mode_q, creating an asymmetry.

finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0007 shows that when csr_we is asserted and csr_addr matches CSR_DCSR, the full write-data is accepted into dcsr_d with only xdebugver and prv fixups, but no check of debug_mode_q. The RISC-V debug specification requires that DCSR is only accessible from debug mode. Because single_step_o (SNIP-0010 line 961) is sourced directly from dcsr_q.step, a successful unauthorized DCSR write lets software enable or disable single-step execution outside of debug mode, affecting the single_step_csr_commit signal used by the commit stage (SNIP-0001).
finding.security_impact: An attacker with sufficient CSR write privilege (e.g., M-mode software, or S-mode if the privilege check is misconfigured) outside of debug mode can set dcsr.step=1 to force the CPU into single-step execution, causing it to halt after every instruction and effectively perform a denial-of-service. Alternatively, clearing dcsr.step while in debug mode single-step can be done without going through the debug interface, undermining debug-mode isolation guarantees.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The chain context does not include the full privilege-level gating logic that precedes csr_we assertion (i.e., whether M-mode-only CSR address encoding for DCSR prevents S/U mode writes). If the CSR address space for DCSR (0x7B0) is already restricted to M-mode by the top-level privilege check before csr_we is set, the impact is reduced to M-mode software only. However, the absence of an explicit debug_mode_q check inside the CSR_DCSR write handler is still a deviation from the RISC-V debug specification.

finding.summary: The `umode_i` input unconditionally elevates privilege level to machine mode (PRIV_LVL_M), bypassing normal privilege tracking, and `sum_o` is derived directly from `mstatus_q.sum` without checking the current privilege level, potentially exposing supervisor user-memory access permission to unprivileged contexts.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` — the `umode_i` input, whose source and authentication are not visible in the provided snippets, unconditionally forces the reported privilege level to machine mode. If `umode_i` can be asserted by unprivileged logic or external input without a corresponding privilege check, any consumer of `priv_lvl_o` (including MMU translation enable logic on line 948) will incorrectly treat the hart as running at M-mode, bypassing S/U mode restrictions.
evidence.supports_claim: Unauthenticated or externally controllable `umode_i` bypasses privilege-level enforcement by forcing PRIV_LVL_M.

evidence.description: Line 946: `assign sum_o = mstatus_q.sum;` — the SUM (Supervisor User Memory) bit is output without gating on the current privilege level. In RISC-V, SUM is only meaningful when the hart is in supervisor mode; outputting it unconditionally means the MMU may permit supervisor-mode access to user pages even when privilege level tracking is corrupted by the `umode_i` bypass.
evidence.supports_claim: sum_o is driven directly from mstatus without privilege-level qualification, compounding the impact of a corrupted priv_lvl_o.

evidence.description: Line 948: `assign en_translation_o = (satp_q.mode == 4'h8 && priv_lvl_o != riscv::PRIV_LVL_M) ? 1'b1 : 1'b0;` — address translation is disabled when priv_lvl_o equals PRIV_LVL_M. Because `umode_i` forces priv_lvl_o to PRIV_LVL_M, asserting `umode_i` disables VA translation entirely, removing page-table-based memory isolation.
evidence.supports_claim: Forcing priv_lvl_o to PRIV_LVL_M via umode_i disables virtual address translation, removing MMU-based memory protection.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `priv_lvl_o` assignment at line 938 treats `umode_i` equivalently to `debug_mode_q`, elevating privilege to M-mode whenever either is asserted. Unlike `debug_mode_q`, which is managed through a controlled debug entry/exit state machine with hardware authentication, `umode_i` is a raw input port with no visible guard in the provided source. This forces the hart's reported privilege level to PRIV_LVL_M, which in turn disables VA translation (line 948), bypassing the MMU's memory isolation. Additionally, `sum_o` at line 946 is derived solely from `mstatus_q.sum` without gating on whether the privilege level is actually supervisor mode, meaning SUM semantics are applied in potentially inappropriate privilege contexts.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured component that can assert `umode_i` gains the ability to: (1) disable virtual address translation entirely, collapsing MMU-based memory isolation; (2) operate at reported machine-mode privilege, bypassing S/U mode access restrictions; (3) potentially leverage the unconditional SUM output to permit cross-boundary memory accesses. This could lead to complete privilege escalation and memory isolation bypass.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source and driving logic of `umode_i` are not visible in the provided snippets — it appears as an input port at line 83 but its instantiation and driver in ariane.sv or a parent module are not included. If `umode_i` is only ever driven by a trusted hardware signal (e.g., a locked configuration fuse), the severity is reduced. The exact RISC-V compliance intention of `umode_i` is also unclear from the available code.

finding.summary: The `priv_lvl_o` output is forced to Machine privilege level (PRIV_LVL_M) when `umode_i` is asserted, without any authentication or debug-mode gating. This means any external signal asserting `umode_i` unconditionally elevates the privilege level to M-mode, bypassing the normal privilege tracking in `priv_lvl_q`.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` — The `umode_i` input is OR'd with `debug_mode_q` to unconditionally force the output privilege level to M-mode. Unlike `debug_mode_q`, which is a carefully managed internal state, `umode_i` is a raw external input with no guard logic shown in the snippets. Any assertion of this signal bypasses the privilege level register.
evidence.supports_claim: An unguarded external input `umode_i` can force privilege level output to PRIV_LVL_M, bypassing the hardware privilege tracking state `priv_lvl_q`, constituting a privilege escalation path.

evidence.description: The module interface at line 83 declares `input logic umode_i` as a plain input port with no documentation comment explaining its intended use or access control. It is treated equivalently to `debug_mode_q` in the privilege output assignment despite having no internal state protection.
evidence.supports_claim: The `umode_i` signal is an unprotected external input that influences the privilege level output directly, with no privilege or lifecycle check visible in the source.

evidence.description: The `priv_lvl_o` signal directly controls CPU privilege level used for translation enable (`en_translation_o` at line 948), TVM/TW/TSR trap controls (lines 952-954), and other security-critical decisions. Forcing it to M-mode disables virtual memory and suppresses trap enforcement.
evidence.supports_claim: Elevating `priv_lvl_o` to M-mode disables address translation and privilege-level enforcement throughout the pipeline, including the TSR (trap-sret) mechanism relevant to this chain.

evidence.description: `tsr_csr_id` is declared as an internal wire in ariane.sv connected to the `tsr_o` output of csr_regfile. The TSR bit (mstatus.tsr) is intended to trap S-mode SRET instructions; its enforcement depends on correct privilege level reporting from `priv_lvl_o`.
evidence.supports_claim: The TSR protection mechanism can be undermined if privilege level is incorrectly reported as M-mode, since TSR trapping only applies to supervisor mode.

finding.reasoning_summary: The CSR register file unconditionally maps `umode_i` to M-mode privilege output on line 938, treating it identically to the guarded `debug_mode_q`. However, `debug_mode_q` is an internal state register with controlled transitions, while `umode_i` is a raw external input port with no visible guard, authentication, or lifecycle check. Asserting `umode_i` forces `priv_lvl_o` to PRIV_LVL_M, which disables virtual memory translation (line 948), suppresses TVM/TW/TSR enforcement (lines 952-954), and affects the `tsr_csr_id` signal in ariane.sv. This constitutes a privilege escalation vulnerability via an unguarded input.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or untrusted component with control over the `umode_i` signal can force the CPU into effective M-mode privilege, bypassing S-mode restrictions, disabling address translation, and suppressing trap-on-SRET (TSR) enforcement. This could allow S-mode code to execute privileged operations, access physical memory directly, and bypass hypervisor-level isolation controls.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The intended semantics of `umode_i` are not documented in the provided snippets. It is possible this input is meant for a specific legitimate microarchitectural use case and is gated externally. However, based solely on the source evidence, no guard logic is visible and the assignment is unconditional. Additionally, the full dataflow from `umode_i` to its external driver is not shown.

finding.summary: The `priv_lvl_o` signal is forced to machine-privilege level (PRIV_LVL_M) when the `umode_i` input is asserted, bypassing the actual privilege-level register. This means any external signal asserting `umode_i` unconditionally elevates privilege to M-mode, regardless of the current privilege state or any authorization check.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` — the `umode_i` input is OR'd with `debug_mode_q` to unconditionally override the privilege level output to PRIV_LVL_M. There is no guard or check on `umode_i`; any assertion of this input grants full machine-mode privilege.
evidence.supports_claim: An unguarded external input `umode_i` can unconditionally elevate the effective privilege level to machine mode, bypassing the privilege register and all privilege-level-based access controls downstream.

evidence.description: The module port declaration shows `umode_i` as a plain `input logic` with no documented privilege requirement or hardware lock on its source (line 83). The privilege level output `priv_lvl_o` (line 50) is the authoritative privilege signal consumed by the rest of the pipeline.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege level output used throughout the system is trivially overrideable via `umode_i` without any authentication or permission check.

evidence.description: Lines 948-950: `en_translation_o` (virtual address translation enable) is gated on `priv_lvl_o != riscv::PRIV_LVL_M`. Asserting `umode_i` forces `priv_lvl_o` to M-mode, which disables address translation, potentially allowing physical memory access without MMU enforcement.
evidence.supports_claim: Forcing M-mode via `umode_i` disables VM address translation, undermining memory isolation guarantees.

evidence.description: The CSR write logic for sensitive supervisor and machine CSRs (e.g., SATP, MSTATUS, SSTATUS) depends on privilege checks enforced via `priv_lvl_o`. If `priv_lvl_o` is artificially elevated to M-mode via `umode_i`, these checks may be bypassed.
evidence.supports_claim: Privilege-level-dependent CSR access guards are undermined when `priv_lvl_o` is overridden by `umode_i`.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `priv_lvl_o` assignment at line 938 of `csr_regfile.sv` shows that asserting `umode_i` (an unguarded input port) forces the privilege level output to PRIV_LVL_M, identical to the debug-mode override. Unlike `debug_mode_q` which is an internal state controlled by a hardware debug protocol, `umode_i` appears to be an unchecked external input with no documented security invariant. This creates a privilege escalation path: any agent that can assert `umode_i` bypasses all privilege-level-based access controls, including address translation gating and CSR access restrictions.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or misconfigured hardware path that can assert `umode_i` gains full machine-mode privilege, disabling virtual memory translation and bypassing CSR access privilege checks, potentially leading to full system compromise including access to all physical memory and machine-mode CSRs.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The source of `umode_i` and how it is driven at the top level (ariane.sv or above) is not shown in the provided snippets. If `umode_i` is itself protected by a hardware lock or driven only from a trusted source, the severity may be reduced. The signal name 'umode_i' is also ambiguous — it may be intended as a legitimate override for a specific use case. Additional context on its driver would increase or decrease confidence.

finding.summary: The priv_lvl_o output forces Machine-level privilege unconditionally when umode_i is asserted, regardless of actual privilege state, potentially allowing privilege escalation via an external input signal.

evidence.description: Line 938: `assign priv_lvl_o = (debug_mode_q || umode_i) ? riscv::PRIV_LVL_M : priv_lvl_q;` — The `umode_i` input is an externally supplied signal that, when asserted, forces the output privilege level to PRIV_LVL_M (Machine mode). This is architecturally anomalous: an input named `umode_i` (suggesting user-mode) that, when true, grants Machine-level privilege rather than user-level privilege. There is no guard or validation of this input, and it can override the normal privilege level state stored in `priv_lvl_q`.
evidence.supports_claim: An unguarded external input `umode_i` unconditionally elevates the reported privilege level to Machine mode, bypassing the normal privilege tracking mechanism.

evidence.description: Line 83: `umode_i` is declared as an input port `input logic umode_i` with no documentation comment explaining its purpose or expected trust boundary. The port is accepted without any validation or gating logic inside the module.
evidence.supports_claim: The `umode_i` signal is an unconstrained external input that feeds directly into privilege level arbitration.

evidence.description: The `priv_lvl_o` signal is used to control address translation enable (`en_translation_o` at line 948 checks `priv_lvl_o != riscv::PRIV_LVL_M`), meaning forcing PRIV_LVL_M via `umode_i` also disables virtual address translation. This is a second-order security impact: any software or hardware that drives `umode_i` high can simultaneously claim M-mode privilege and disable MMU translation.
evidence.supports_claim: Privilege escalation through `umode_i` also silently disables address translation, compounding the security impact.

evidence.description: Signals `tvm_csr_id`, `tw_csr_id`, and `tsr_csr_id` in ariane.sv (lines 182-184) are tied to CSR privilege-control outputs (tvm_o, tw_o, tsr_o from csr_regfile). These controls govern virtual memory trap behavior and WFI trap behavior, all of which depend on correct privilege level reporting that can be corrupted by `umode_i`.
evidence.supports_claim: The privilege level corruption via `umode_i` affects downstream privilege-sensitive controls including TVM, TW, and TSR.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `priv_lvl_o` assignment in csr_regfile.sv line 938 combines `debug_mode_q` (a legitimate internal state) with `umode_i` (an external input) using OR logic, both of which force PRIV_LVL_M. While `debug_mode_q` being used this way is expected (debug mode runs at M-level), the `umode_i` signal has no such justification — its name suggests user mode but it produces the opposite effect. There is no filtering, trust-domain validation, or documentation for this input. Any hardware or software that can assert `umode_i` can claim Machine privilege level and simultaneously disable MMU translation, bypassing the normal privilege state machine tracked by `priv_lvl_q`.
finding.security_impact: An attacker or malicious hardware component capable of asserting `umode_i` can force the CPU to report Machine-level privilege to all downstream consumers (MMU, trap logic, CSR access checks), disable address translation, and bypass privilege-gated behaviors — effectively achieving full privilege escalation without going through normal exception or mode-change paths.
finding.uncertainty_or_missing_evidence: The intended semantics of `umode_i` are not documented in the available source. However, no guard logic, comment, or constraint is visible in the provided snippets to justify the M-mode override behavior. Any assertion of `umode_i` bypasses the stored privilege level `priv_lvl_q`, granting M-mode privilege without any guard, authentication check, or lock.

evidence.supports_claim: umode_i is an external, unprotected input that feeds directly into privilege level output logic.

evidence.supports_claim: Forcing PRIV_LVL_M via umode_i also disables MMU translation, compounding the security impact.

finding.reasoning_summary: The `umode_i` signal is an external input to `csr_regfile` that, when asserted, forces `priv_lvl_o` to `PRIV_LVL_M` (Machine mode) regardless of the actual hardware privilege state stored in `priv_lvl_q`. This is done with the same logic path as `debug_mode_q`, but `debug_mode_q` is an internally managed debug state. `umode_i` is a module port driven externally with no visible guard, authentication, or qualification. finding.reasoning_summary: SNIP-0015 shows the privilege level check for CSR access. With this bypass, a user or supervisor mode program can directly read or modify the machine exception program counter, which undermines the privilege isolation guarantees of the RISC-V ISA.
